title: Birthday Problem

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: Your boss A's birthday is one of these 10 dates: Mar 4, Mar 5, Mar 8, Jun 4, Jun 7, Sep 1, Sep 5, Dec 1, Dec 2, Dec 8.
rules: You are told only the MONTH of A's birthday.
Your colleague C is told only the DAY.
You then say: 'I don't know A's birthday; C doesn't know it either.'
C replies: 'I didn't know A's birthday before, but now I know it.'
You say: 'Now I know it too.'
setup.question: What is A's birthday?

learningObjectives: Recognise that ignorance statements ('I don't know') carry information about the possibility space.
Apply each statement as a filter and track what survives.
Distinguish 'I know' from 'someone else can't know'.

stages.label: Comprehension
stages.prompt: Which two days appear in the list exactly once? (These are the days that, if C were told them, would let C know the birthday immediately.)
stages.explanation: Listing days: 1 (Sep 1, Dec 1), 2 (Dec 2), 4 (Mar 4, Jun 4), 5 (Mar 5, Sep 5), 7 (Jun 7), 8 (Mar 8, Dec 8). Days 2 and 7 each appear only once.

stages.label: Choose your technique
stages.prompt: What's the right framing for this problem?

options.text: Logic reasoning -- treat each statement as a filter on the 10 possible dates
There's not enough information; you need extra data

stages.explanation: Each statement -- including the ignorance statements -- eliminates a subset of the 10 dates. Apply them one at a time. The last surviving date is the answer.

stages.label: Apply Statement 1
stages.prompt: You said: 'I don't know A's birthday; AND C doesn't know it either.' You only know the month -- so the second clause means: the month you were told contains NO unique-day. Which months are eliminated?

options.text: March and September (the months with day 5)
June and December (because June contains day 7, and December contains day 2)
None -- nothing can be eliminated yet

stages.explanation: If your month were June or December, you couldn't be SURE that C doesn't know. (C might have been told day 7 or day 2 -- in which case C would know.) Your certainty that C is in the dark means your month contains no unique day. June (has 7) and December (has 2) are out. Surviving months: March, September. Surviving dates: Mar 4, Mar 5, Mar 8, Sep 1, Sep 5.

stages.label: Apply Statement 2
stages.prompt: C now says: 'I know it.' From the 5 surviving dates {Mar 4, Mar 5, Mar 8, Sep 1, Sep 5}, which days are still ambiguous to C (so C wouldn't know)?

options.text: Day 4 (Mar 4 still has March as another option)

stages.explanation: Day 5 appears twice in the surviving set (Mar 5 and Sep 5). If C had been told day 5, C would still not know. C says 'I know it' -- so the day is NOT 5. Mar 5 and Sep 5 are eliminated. Surviving: Mar 4, Mar 8, Sep 1.

stages.label: Apply Statement 3
stages.prompt: You now say: 'Now I know it too.' From {Mar 4, Mar 8, Sep 1}, which month would let YOU narrow down to exactly one date?

options.text: March (Mar 4 and Mar 8 still both survive -- you wouldn't know)

stages.explanation: If your month were March, you'd still see TWO surviving dates (Mar 4 and Mar 8) and couldn't claim to know. Your saying 'now I know' means your month must be one with exactly one surviving date -- September, with only Sep 1 left.

stages.label: Pattern
stages.prompt: Fill in the meta-pattern that powered this whole solution.
stages.template: An 'I don't know' statement eliminates possibilities where the speaker WOULD have __[a]__. An 'I know now' statement eliminates possibilities where the speaker would still be __[b]__.
stages.explanation: This is the meta-information principle. Every utterance about knowledge -- positive or negative -- is itself data about the underlying possibility space. Cheryl's-Birthday-style puzzles all use this.

stages.label: Commit final answer
stages.explanation: September 1. Each of the three statements pruned the possibility set: 10 dates -> 5 -> 3 -> 1.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: In one sentence, what was the load-bearing insight that made the first elimination possible?
Could the puzzle still be solved if the speakers' order were swapped (C goes first)?
What's the smallest set of dates that would still admit a unique solution under this exact dialogue?
